meta.title: Mercedes S350d S400d Engine Replacement Cost UK — Compare Quotes
meta.description: Every Mercedes‑Benz S350d / S400d engine — OM656 2.9 straight-six diesel. W222 and W223 generations, real UK costs and reliability. Get free quotes from 25+ vetted Mercedes specialists today.

@graph.name: Mercedes‑Benz S350d / S400d Engine Era Map

mainEntity.name: How much does a Mercedes‑Benz S350d / S400d engine replacement cost in the UK?

acceptedAnswer.text: A reconditioned OM656 engine typically costs £3,500–£6,500 supply only for the S350d, and £4,000–£8,000 for the S400d. Fitting adds £1,000–£1,600. Camshaft and rocker wear repairs can cost £1,500–£3,500 if caught early. Use our calculator for a figure specific to your car.

mainEntity.name: Is the Mercedes‑Benz S350d / S400d engine reliable?

acceptedAnswer.text: The OM656 is rated ★★★★☆ — it's a major step forward from the OM642 V6 in refinement and efficiency. However, it has known issues: turbo actuator vacuum hose collapse (particularly on the S400d), camshaft rocker wear (around 100,000 miles), AdBlue system faults, DPF clogging, and vacuum pump oil leaks on early cars. The fuel system is sensitive to fuel quality.

mainEntity.name: What are the common problems with the Mercedes‑Benz S350d / S400d?

acceptedAnswer.text: Turbo actuator vacuum hose collapse (intermittent power loss, gearbox judder), camshaft rocker wear (popping noise from intake), AdBlue system faults (NOx sensors, pump), DPF clogging, vacuum pump oil leaks (early cars), and fuel system contamination. The turbo hose issue is particularly common on the S400d.

mainEntity.name: Is it worth replacing a Mercedes‑Benz S350d / S400d engine, or should I scrap the car?

acceptedAnswer.text: Depends on vehicle value. Against a well‑kept W223 S400d worth £40,000–£70,000, a £4,000–£8,000 recon is a manageable 6–20% of the vehicle's value — worth it. For a W222 S350d worth £15,000–£25,000, a £4,500 recon is more marginal but often worthwhile given the S-Class's status and retained values.

mainEntity.name: How do I find my Mercedes‑Benz S350d / S400d engine code?

acceptedAnswer.text: Listed on the VIN plate and vehicle documentation. The S350d and S400d both use the OM656 2.9-litre inline-six diesel engine. Tell us your registration date and we'll confirm it for free.

mainEntity.name: Can I repair the OM656's turbo actuator hose, or does it need a new engine?

acceptedAnswer.text: Yes — turbo actuator hose replacement is a straightforward repair costing £300–£800. Mercedes is investigating a factory fix for the collapsing hose issue. This does not typically require engine replacement. At the first sign of power loss or gearbox judder, book an inspection immediately.

eraMap.title: The S350d / S400d THROUGH TIME — Era Map

rows.eraNote: First-generation OM656 in the S-Class — robust and refined, but watch for turbo actuator vacuum hose collapse and cam rocker wear.
The modern S350d — improved OM656 with 48V mild-hybrid; AdBlue system faults and valvetrain wear are the main watchpoints.
The higher-output OM656 — 330 bhp, available with 4MATIC; same engine architecture with higher boost and more emissions complexity.

replacementCosts.h2: Mercedes-Benz S350d / S400d Engine Replacement Costs
replacementCosts.labourEstimate: Fitting an S350d / S400d engine typically takes 12–16 hours at £65–£95/hr (independent) or £110–£160/hr (London/SE dealer). Camshaft and rocker wear repairs require cylinder head work and are expensive.

repairBuyOrReplace.h2: Mercedes-Benz S350d / S400d — Repair, Buy, or Replace?

rows.whenItMakesSense: Replace with updated hose; common on S400d
If caught early; don't drive with intake popping
Replace NOx sensors, pump or injector; don't let countdown expire
If it hasn't happened by 40k miles, it won't
Preventative — regular motorway runs are the best prevention
Use high-quality diesel and regular filter changes

buyingChecks.title: Buying This S350d / S400d? Check These First
buyIf: * Full Mercedes service history with documented oil changes
* Post-2020 W223 — generally more refined than the W222
* No AdBlue system issues or evidence of NOx sensor replacement
* Vacuum pump oil leak not present (if it hasn't happened by 40k, it won't)
* No intake popping noise — indicates rocker wear
avoidIf: * Intermittent power loss or gearbox judder — potential turbo actuator hose issue
* Intake popping noise — camshaft rocker wear warning
* AdBlue countdown active — potential system failure
* Poor service history — the OM656 is sensitive to fuel and oil quality
* High-mileage example (100k+) without documented valvetrain inspection

repairBuyOrReplace.closingVerdict: The S350d / S400d's OM656 is Mercedes' most refined diesel engine — a smooth, powerful straight-six that delivers effortless performance in the flagship S-Class. It's a significant step forward from the OM642 V6 in refinement and efficiency. However, it has known issues: turbo actuator vacuum hose collapse (particularly on the S400d), camshaft rocker wear around 100,000 miles, and AdBlue system faults. Buy on documented history, and if you're looking at a high-mileage example, demand proof of valvetrain inspection. If you're replacing, a reconditioned unit with a 12‑month warranty is the sweet spot.

buyingChecklist.intro: Before you buy a used Mercedes‑Benz S350d / S400d (or before you commit to an engine replacement), check these:
items: * Turbo Health — Test acceleration; any intermittent power loss or judder? Turbo actuator vacuum hose collapse risk
* Intake Noise — Listen for popping noise from the intake — camshaft rocker wear warning
* Cold Start — Any rattling or ticking? Valvetrain wear indicator
* Service History — Including which engine era (OM656 is the only S350d/S400d engine — verify this before purchase) and fuel filter change history
* AdBlue System — Any AdBlue warnings? Countdown active? NOx sensor or pump failure
* Oil Leaks — Check for oil spots under the car — vacuum pump oil leak (early cars)
* DPF Status — Any DPF warning lights or regeneration issues?
* Engine Warning Light — Have codes been read? Any unresolved turbo, AdBlue, or emissions issues?
